message.text: S3 bucket example: at least one Block Public Access flag is disabled. Public-access risk is not fully mitigated.
S3 bucket km_public_blob: at least one Block Public Access flag is disabled. Public-access risk is not fully mitigated.
S3 bucket policy on b does not reference aws:SecureTransport — traffic can potentially be accepted over plain HTTP.
S3 bucket mybucket: no aws_s3_bucket_public_access_block references this bucket. Public access posture is one mis-applied policy or ACL away.
S3 bucket km_blob_storage: no aws_s3_bucket_public_access_block references this bucket. Public access posture is one mis-applied policy or ACL away.
RDS instance should not be publicly accessible: cannot verify — required attribute(s) missing from source: publicly_accessible
RDS instance should have automated backups enabled (backup_retention_period > 0): cannot verify — required attribute(s) missing from source: backup_retention_period
RDS instance km_db has multi_az = false. A single-AZ failure will cause an outage for the duration of cross-AZ recovery (typically 30-90 minutes).
RDS instance km_db: deletion_protection is disabled. An accidental terraform destroy will permanently delete the database.
EC2 instance web-app-instance: IMDSv2 is not required (http_tokens != "required"). SSRF-based credential theft via IMDSv1 remains possible.
EC2 instance privileged-instance: IMDSv2 is not required (http_tokens != "required"). SSRF-based credential theft via IMDSv1 remains possible.
EC2 instance should have EBS root volume encryption enabled: cannot verify — required attribute(s) missing from source: root_block_device.encrypted
EC2 instance web-app-instance: root EBS volume is not encrypted. OS-level data and cached credentials are in cleartext at rest.
EC2 instance privileged-instance: root EBS volume is not encrypted. OS-level data and cached credentials are in cleartext at rest.
EC2 instance should have EBS root volume encryption enabled: cannot verify — required attribute(s) missing from source: root_block_device.encrypted
EC2 instance km_vm: no metadata_options block declared. AWS defaults to http_tokens = "optional" (IMDSv1 enabled). SSRF-based credential theft via the instance metadata service remains possible.

EC2 instance hybrid_node: no metadata_options block declared. AWS defaults to http_tokens = "optional" (IMDSv1 enabled). SSRF-based credential theft via the instance metadata service remains possible.

EC2 instance web-app-instance: monitoring is explicitly set to false, disabling detailed CloudWatch metrics. 1-minute granularity metrics are unavailable — CPU spikes from crypto-mining or DDoS are visible only at 5-minute intervals, inflating mean-time-to-detect by up to 5×.

EC2 instance privileged-instance: monitoring is explicitly set to false, disabling detailed CloudWatch metrics. 1-minute granularity metrics are unavailable — CPU spikes from crypto-mining or DDoS are visible only at 5-minute intervals, inflating mean-time-to-detect by up to 5×.
KMS key km_db_kms_key: deletion_window_in_days is less than the maximum of 30. If scheduled for deletion, there is less time to cancel and recover data encrypted with this key.
ECS task definition km_ecs_task: at least one container does not set readonlyRootFilesystem = true. Compromised containers can write payloads/persistence to the runtime image.
ECS cluster does not enable Container Insights: cannot verify — required attribute(s) missing from source: setting
ECS task definition km_ecs_task: task_role_arn is not set. The container falls back to the EC2 instance profile (or no role on Fargate) — both are wrong defaults.
ECS cluster km_ecs_cluster: execute-command configuration is missing — when operators run `aws ecs execute-command`, sessions are not logged. Investigations of insider misuse become unreliable.
EKS cluster this: no encryption_config block. Kubernetes Secrets are stored unencrypted in etcd — anyone with etcd-level access (or backup access) reads them in plaintext.
EKS cluster this: control-plane logging does not include `audit`. Forensic investigation of cluster-level access (kubectl, CRD operations, RBAC misuse) is impossible after the fact.
VPC km_vpc: no aws_flow_log resource references this VPC. Network-level forensics (lateral movement, exfiltration) are unreliable post-incident.
VPC demo-vpc: no aws_flow_log resource references this VPC. Network-level forensics (lateral movement, exfiltration) are unreliable post-incident.

Security group km_rds_sg: ingress allows 0.0.0.0/0 on a known debug/management port (e.g., 8080 / 5432 / 9200 / 6379). These are admin-tool defaults — exposing them to the internet is almost always unintended.
Security group permits 0.0.0.0/0 on a known debug or management port: cannot verify — required attribute(s) missing from source: ingress.cidr_blocks
Security group permits 0.0.0.0/0 on a known debug or management port: cannot verify — required attribute(s) missing from source: ingress.cidr_blocks
Security group permits 0.0.0.0/0 on a known debug or management port: cannot verify — required attribute(s) missing from source: ingress.cidr_blocks
Security group permits 0.0.0.0/0 on a known debug or management port: cannot verify — required attribute(s) missing from source: ingress.cidr_blocks
Security group permits 0.0.0.0/0 on a known debug or management port: cannot verify — required attribute(s) missing from source: ingress.cidr_blocks

Subnet km_public_subnet: map_public_ip_on_launch = true. Every instance launched into this subnet receives a public IP automatically — including future instances created by ASGs, ECS, or EKS without an explicit private-only override.
SQS queue this: no redrive_policy declared. Messages that fail to process retry until message_retention_seconds expires, then drop silently.
ALB km_lb: no alarm references this load balancer. Target health is a load-balancer concern — without a paging hook, a target group running with 50% healthy hosts is silent until the last host fails.

RDS km_db: no alarms reference this instance. CPU saturation and connection-pool exhaustion are the two failure modes engineers don't catch until customers complain. Both have CloudWatch metrics; neither has a configured threshold here.

SQS queue this: no alarms reference this queue. Consumer death is invisible from the queue side until messages back up. ApproximateAgeOfOldestMessage exceeding a few minutes is unambiguous evidence the consumer stopped processing.
EKS cluster this: <= 1 aws_eks_node_group references this cluster. Single-pool clusters can't drain workloads across pools during a kernel upgrade or instance-family migration.
ECS service km_ecs_service: desired_count = 1. No redundancy across tasks; rolling deploys are not actually rolling — they're sequential blackouts.
RDS km_db: max_allocated_storage unset. The instance can't grow beyond its allocated_storage value. A surprise write spike → ENOSPC → forced downtime to bump storage manually.
IAM policy km_ssm_secrets_policy: combines Resource: "*" with sensitive action prefixes. The blast radius of a token compromise is the entire account for those service classes.
IAM policy web-app-instance-policy: combines Resource: "*" with sensitive action prefixes. The blast radius of a token compromise is the entire account for those service classes.
IAM policy km_ssm_secrets_policy: grants a high-privilege action with no Condition block. Compromise of any principal holding this policy gives unconstrained use of that action.
IAM policy cluster_encryption: grants a high-privilege action with no Condition block. Compromise of any principal holding this policy gives unconstrained use of that action.
Elastic IP declared without instance / NAT / NLB association: cannot verify — required attribute(s) missing from source: domain

NAT Gateway km_nat_gateway: declared. If your plan has multiple of these in the same VPC (one per AZ), the redundancy costs $32/month per extra AZ — fine for prod with steady traffic, but commonly accidental in dev/staging.
EventBridge rule with rate(1 minute) schedule (high-cost cron): cannot verify — required attribute(s) missing from source: name
mybucket: no lifecycle.prevent_destroy block. A terraform destroy or refactor that removes this resource address will delete it without a guardrail.
km_db: no lifecycle.prevent_destroy block. A terraform destroy or refactor that removes this resource address will delete it without a guardrail.
km_blob_storage: no lifecycle.prevent_destroy block. A terraform destroy or refactor that removes this resource address will delete it without a guardrail.
km_public_blob: no lifecycle.prevent_destroy block. A terraform destroy or refactor that removes this resource address will delete it without a guardrail.
this: uses `count = condition ? 1 : 0` pattern. Changes to the condition logic can renumber the resource's address, causing terraform to plan a delete+create instead of an update.
eks_auto: uses `count = condition ? 1 : 0` pattern. Changes to the condition logic can renumber the resource's address, causing terraform to plan a delete+create instead of an update.

this: uses `count = condition ? 1 : 0` pattern. Changes to the condition logic can renumber the resource's address, causing terraform to plan a delete+create instead of an update.
VPC km_vpc: no aws_vpc_endpoint declared. Secrets Manager calls from in-VPC workloads traverse NAT. At ~$0.045/GB through NAT, even modest secret-fetching patterns pay back the endpoint cost in days.

VPC demo-vpc: no aws_vpc_endpoint declared. Secrets Manager calls from in-VPC workloads traverse NAT. At ~$0.045/GB through NAT, even modest secret-fetching patterns pay back the endpoint cost in days.
VPC km_vpc: no aws_vpc_endpoint for KMS. Decrypt calls leave the VPC via NAT — for high-volume workloads this adds up faster than the endpoint cost.
VPC demo-vpc: no aws_vpc_endpoint for KMS. Decrypt calls leave the VPC via NAT — for high-volume workloads this adds up faster than the endpoint cost.
Cluster km_ecs_cluster: no aws_vpc_endpoint declared for ECR or S3. Container image pulls + Docker layer downloads flow through NAT — common single-biggest contributor to NAT data-processing charges.
Cluster this: no aws_vpc_endpoint declared for ECR or S3. Container image pulls + Docker layer downloads flow through NAT — common single-biggest contributor to NAT data-processing charges.
EKS cluster this: enabled_cluster_log_types missing `audit`. Cluster-level API access is invisible — no record of who called what during an incident or audit.
km_ecs_cluster: no aws_inspector2_enabler in the plan. Container / runtime / EC2 CVEs aren't surfaced — the team is doing vulnerability management manually or not at all.
this: no aws_inspector2_enabler in the plan. Container / runtime / EC2 CVEs aren't surfaced — the team is doing vulnerability management manually or not at all.
Load balancer km_lb: access logging is not enabled. Request-level forensics (source IP, path, status, latency, backend) are unavailable after an incident — you cannot reconstruct who hit what.
ALB km_lb: drop_invalid_header_fields is false. Malformed HTTP headers (e.g. header injection, request smuggling via ambiguous Content-Length) are forwarded to backend targets unchanged.

ALB km_lb: deletion protection is off. The load balancer can be deleted by any IAM principal with elasticloadbalancing:DeleteLoadBalancer — including a misconfigured CI pipeline or confused-deputy attack.

ALB listener km_frontend_listener: protocol is HTTP and default action is not a redirect to HTTPS. Client connections are accepted over plaintext — session tokens, credentials, and API responses are transmitted unencrypted.

IAM policy web-app-instance-policy: enables privilege escalation — set own IAM policy version; attach an administrator policy; add an inline admin policy; create access keys for another user; set a console login password; add a principal to a group; rewrite a role's trust policy; pass any role to a compute service; create a new role with admin policy; remove permissions boundary to escalate — granted on an unconstrained resource (Resource: "*") with no Condition. Any principal holding this policy can escalate to broader or administrator privileges.

IAM policy privileged-instance-policy: enables privilege escalation — full administrator wildcard (Action: "*") — granted on an unconstrained resource (Resource: "*") with no Condition. Any principal holding this policy can escalate to broader or administrator privileges.

Toxic combination on role web-app-instance-role: it is assumable by a compute service (EC2 / ECS / Lambda), AND its attached policies grant set own IAM policy version; attach an administrator policy; add an inline admin policy; create access keys for another user; set a console login password; add a principal to a group; rewrite a role's trust policy; pass any role to a compute service; create a new role with admin policy; remove permissions boundary to escalate. An attacker who compromises that workload (SSRF, RCE, dependency takeover) can steal the instance/task role credentials and obtain those permissions. Neither the trust policy nor the permission policy is flagged in isolation — the risk is the path between them.

Toxic combination on role privileged-instance-role: it is assumable by a compute service (EC2 / ECS / Lambda), AND its attached policies grant full administrator wildcard (Action: "*"). An attacker who compromises that workload (SSRF, RCE, dependency takeover) can steal the instance/task role credentials and obtain those permissions. Neither the trust policy nor the permission policy is flagged in isolation — the risk is the path between them.

EC2 instance web-app-instance: IMDSv2 is not enforced (metadata_options.http_tokens ≠ "required") and the attached instance profile role `web-app-instance-role` grants set own IAM policy version; attach an administrator policy; add an inline admin policy; create access keys for another user; set a console login password; add a principal to a group; rewrite a role's trust policy; pass any role to a compute service; create a new role with admin policy; remove permissions boundary to escalate. Any server-side request forgery (SSRF) vulnerability in the running application can steal the role credentials in one unauthenticated HTTP request to 169.254.169.254, then immediately use them to set own IAM policy version; attach an administrator policy; add an inline admin policy; create access keys for another user; set a console login password; add a principal to a group; rewrite a role's trust policy; pass any role to a compute service; create a new role with admin policy; remove permissions boundary to escalate.

EC2 instance privileged-instance: IMDSv2 is not enforced (metadata_options.http_tokens ≠ "required") and the attached instance profile role `privileged-instance-role` grants full administrator wildcard (Action: "*"). Any server-side request forgery (SSRF) vulnerability in the running application can steal the role credentials in one unauthenticated HTTP request to 169.254.169.254, then immediately use them to full administrator wildcard (Action: "*").

EC2 instance `web-app-instance` has security group `web-app-security-group` with 0.0.0.0/0 ingress on SSH/RDP, and its instance profile role `web-app-instance-role` grants set own IAM policy version; attach an administrator policy; add an inline admin policy; create access keys for another user; set a console login password; add a principal to a group; rewrite a role's trust policy; pass any role to a compute service; create a new role with admin policy; remove permissions boundary to escalate. An attacker who gains shell access can query the EC2 Instance Metadata Service (IMDS) for temporary credentials and use them to escalate IAM privileges.

EC2 instance `privileged-instance` has security group `web-app-security-group` with 0.0.0.0/0 ingress on SSH/RDP, and its instance profile role `privileged-instance-role` grants full administrator wildcard (Action: "*"). An attacker who gains shell access can query the EC2 Instance Metadata Service (IMDS) for temporary credentials and use them to gain full administrative control of the AWS account.

Attack path (3 hops: SSRF → IMDS credential theft → IAM privilege escalation):
[1/3] Entry: EC2 instance `web-app-instance` is reachable from the internet via security group `web-app-security-group` (HTTP/HTTPS port open to 0.0.0.0/0). Any SSRF or injection vulnerability in the running application can reach 169.254.169.254.
[2/3] Pivot: IMDSv2 is NOT enforced (metadata_options.http_tokens = optional). A single unauthenticated HTTP GET to the instance metadata endpoint yields temporary credentials for instance profile role `web-app-instance-role`.
[3/3] Impact: Role `web-app-instance-role` grants set own IAM policy version; attach an administrator policy; add an inline admin policy; create access keys for another user; set a console login password; add a principal to a group; rewrite a role's trust policy; pass any role to a compute service; create a new role with admin policy; remove permissions boundary to escalate — the attacker can use the stolen credentials to escalate IAM privileges.

Attack path (3 hops: SSRF → IMDS credential theft → full account control):
[1/3] Entry: EC2 instance `privileged-instance` is reachable from the internet via security group `web-app-security-group` (HTTP/HTTPS port open to 0.0.0.0/0). Any SSRF or injection vulnerability in the running application can reach 169.254.169.254.
[2/3] Pivot: IMDSv2 is NOT enforced (metadata_options.http_tokens = optional). A single unauthenticated HTTP GET to the instance metadata endpoint yields temporary credentials for instance profile role `privileged-instance-role`.
[3/3] Impact: Role `privileged-instance-role` grants full administrator wildcard (Action: "*") — the attacker can use the stolen credentials to gain full administrative control of the AWS account.

IAM policy privileged-instance-policy: grants data exfiltration actions (s3:GetObject, dynamodb:Scan, dynamodb:GetItem, dynamodb:BatchGetItem, dynamodb:Query, kinesis:GetRecords, sqs:ReceiveMessage, rds:DownloadDBLogFilePortion, athena:GetQueryResults, glue:GetSecurityConfiguration) on Resource: "*" with no Condition. Any principal holding this policy can bulk-read or export data across all accessible resources of that service — without needing to escalate privileges first.

IAM policy km_ssm_secrets_policy: grants credential extraction actions (secretsmanager:GetSecretValue, ssm:GetParameter, ssm:GetParameters, kms:Decrypt) on Resource: "*" with no Condition. Any principal holding this policy can read secrets, SSM parameters, or decrypt KMS-protected data across all resources in the account.

IAM policy privileged-instance-policy: grants credential extraction actions (secretsmanager:GetSecretValue, secretsmanager:BatchGetSecretValue, ssm:GetParameter, ssm:GetParameters, ssm:GetParametersByPath, kms:Decrypt, kms:GenerateDataKey, sts:GetSessionToken, sts:GetFederationToken) on Resource: "*" with no Condition. Any principal holding this policy can read secrets, SSM parameters, or decrypt KMS-protected data across all resources in the account.

IAM policy privileged-instance-policy: grants resource-exposure actions (s3:PutBucketPolicy, s3:PutBucketAcl, s3:PutObjectAcl, lambda:AddPermission, lambda:AddLayerVersionPermission, sqs:AddPermission, sns:AddPermission, secretsmanager:PutResourcePolicy, kms:PutKeyPolicy, ecr:SetRepositoryPolicy) on Resource: "*" with no Condition. Any principal holding this policy can modify the access policy of S3 buckets, Lambda functions, SQS queues, KMS keys, or other resources — potentially making them publicly accessible.

Toxic combination on role km_ecs_task_execution_role: it is assumable by a compute service (EC2 / ECS / Lambda), AND its attached policies grant credential extraction actions (secretsmanager:GetSecretValue, ssm:GetParameter, ssm:GetParameters, kms:Decrypt) on Resource: "*" with no Condition. An attacker who compromises that workload (SSRF, RCE, dependency takeover) can steal the instance/task role credentials and obtain those permissions. An attacker who assumes this role can harvest every secret, SSM parameter, and KMS-encrypted value in the account in a single session.

IAM policy web-app-instance-policy: grants infrastructure-modification actions (ec2:RunInstances, ec2:RequestSpotInstances, ec2:AuthorizeSecurityGroupIngress, ec2:CreateVpc, ec2:CreateKeyPair) on Resource: "*" with no Condition. Any principal holding this policy can launch EC2 instances, run ECS tasks, or modify network rules without restriction — a common cryptomining and lateral-movement vector.

IAM policy privileged-instance-policy: grants infrastructure-modification actions (ec2:RunInstances, ec2:RequestSpotInstances, ecs:RunTask, eks:CreateCluster, rds:CreateDBInstance, rds:RestoreDBInstanceFromDBSnapshot, ec2:AuthorizeSecurityGroupIngress, ec2:CreateVpc, ec2:CreateKeyPair) on Resource: "*" with no Condition. Any principal holding this policy can launch EC2 instances, run ECS tasks, or modify network rules without restriction — a common cryptomining and lateral-movement vector.

Plan includes hourly-billed resources (1 aws_autoscaling_group, 1 aws_db_instance, 1 aws_eks_cluster, 1 aws_nat_gateway, 4× aws_instance) but no aws_budgets_budget. Estimated monthly spend from priceable resources in this plan: ~$155/month (us-east-1 on-demand). Without a cost alarm, a forgotten instance, misconfigured autoscaling, or credential compromise can run up an unbounded bill before the team notices.

S3 buckets (mybucket, km_blob_storage, km_public_blob) have no aws_s3_bucket_lifecycle_configuration. Without lifecycle rules, objects accumulate forever: old versions from versioning, abandoned multipart uploads, and stale data all bill at $0.023/GB-month with no bound.

Plan includes aws_nat_gateway.km_nat_gateway and 3 S3 buckets (mybucket, km_blob_storage, km_public_blob) but no aws_vpc_endpoint for S3. All S3 API calls from private subnets flow through the NAT gateway at $0.045/GB data-processing charge. An S3 Gateway endpoint is completely free and eliminates that cost entirely.

`aws_instance.web-app-instance` has a public IP and an IAM instance profile, the plan defines admin-equivalent IAM policy/policies (web-app-instance-policy, privileged-instance-policy), and there is NO WAF web ACL. An SSRF or RCE on this internet-reachable host reaches the instance metadata service, steals the admin-grade role credentials, and pivots to full account control — with no WAF in front to blunt the initial exploit. Each fact is individually common; together they form a complete breach chain.

`aws_instance.privileged-instance` has a public IP and an IAM instance profile, the plan defines admin-equivalent IAM policy/policies (web-app-instance-policy, privileged-instance-policy), and there is NO WAF web ACL. An SSRF or RCE on this internet-reachable host reaches the instance metadata service, steals the admin-grade role credentials, and pivots to full account control — with no WAF in front to blunt the initial exploit. Each fact is individually common; together they form a complete breach chain.
Role is declared but no other resource references it. Either delete it or document why it's kept (e.g., AssumeRole from another account).

IAM role km_ecs_task_execution_role: permissions_boundary unset. Future policy attachments can grant unlimited privilege — the role's effective permissions = union of all attached policies, no ceiling.
IAM role web-app-instance-role: permissions_boundary unset. Future policy attachments can grant unlimited privilege — the role's effective permissions = union of all attached policies, no ceiling.

fullDescription.text:
Attack path (3 hops: SSRF → IMDS credential theft → IAM privilege escalation):
[1/3] Entry: EC2 instance `web-app-instance` is reachable from the internet via security group `web-app-security-group` (HTTP/HTTPS port open to 0.0.0.0/0). Any SSRF or injection vulnerability in the running application can reach 169.254.169.254.
[2/3] Pivot: IMDSv2 is NOT enforced (metadata_options.http_tokens = optional). A single unauthenticated HTTP GET to the instance metadata endpoint yields temporary credentials for instance profile role `web-app-instance-role`.
[3/3] Impact: Role `web-app-instance-role` grants set own IAM policy version; attach an administrator policy; add an inline admin policy; create access keys for another user; set a console login password; add a principal to a group; rewrite a role's trust policy; pass any role to a compute service; create a new role with admin policy; remove permissions boundary to escalate — the attacker can use the stolen credentials to escalate IAM privileges.

shortDescription.text: Internet-facing EC2 + IMDSv1 + escalation-capable instance profile → SSRF credential theft (IAM privilege escalation)

fullDescription.text: Plan includes hourly-billed resources (1 aws_autoscaling_group, 1 aws_db_instance, 1 aws_eks_cluster, 1 aws_nat_gateway, 4× aws_instance) but no aws_budgets_budget. Estimated monthly spend from priceable resources in this plan: ~$155/month (us-east-1 on-demand). Without a cost alarm, a forgotten instance, misconfigured autoscaling, or credential compromise can run up an unbounded bill before the team notices.

shortDescription.text: No AWS Budget alarm in the Terraform plan

fullDescription.text: S3 buckets (mybucket, km_blob_storage, km_public_blob) have no aws_s3_bucket_lifecycle_configuration. Without lifecycle rules, objects accumulate forever: old versions from versioning, abandoned multipart uploads, and stale data all bill at $0.023/GB-month with no bound.

shortDescription.text: 3 S3 buckets without a lifecycle configuration

fullDescription.text: Plan includes aws_nat_gateway.km_nat_gateway and 3 S3 buckets (mybucket, km_blob_storage, km_public_blob) but no aws_vpc_endpoint for S3. All S3 API calls from private subnets flow through the NAT gateway at $0.045/GB data-processing charge. An S3 Gateway endpoint is completely free and eliminates that cost entirely.

shortDescription.text: NAT Gateway routes S3 traffic — add a free S3 Gateway endpoint

fullDescription.text: EC2 instance web-app-instance: IMDSv2 is not required (http_tokens != "required"). SSRF-based credential theft via IMDSv1 remains possible.

shortDescription.text: EC2 instance should require IMDSv2 (token-based metadata access)

fullDescription.text: EC2 instance should have EBS root volume encryption enabled: cannot verify — required attribute(s) missing from source: root_block_device.encrypted

shortDescription.text: [Needs Review] EC2 instance should have EBS root volume encryption enabled

fullDescription.text: EC2 instance km_vm: no metadata_options block declared. AWS defaults to http_tokens = "optional" (IMDSv1 enabled). SSRF-based credential theft via the instance metadata service remains possible.

shortDescription.text: EC2 instance has no metadata_options block — IMDSv2 not configured

fullDescription.text: EC2 instance `web-app-instance` has security group `web-app-security-group` with 0.0.0.0/0 ingress on SSH/RDP, and its instance profile role `web-app-instance-role` grants set own IAM policy version; attach an administrator policy; add an inline admin policy; create access keys for another user; set a console login password; add a principal to a group; rewrite a role's trust policy; pass any role to a compute service; create a new role with admin policy; remove permissions boundary to escalate. An attacker who gains shell access can query the EC2 Instance Metadata Service (IMDS) for temporary credentials and use them to escalate IAM privileges.

shortDescription.text: EC2 instance reachable via SSH/RDP from internet and attached instance profile is privileged

fullDescription.text: EC2 instance web-app-instance: monitoring is explicitly set to false, disabling detailed CloudWatch metrics. 1-minute granularity metrics are unavailable — CPU spikes from crypto-mining or DDoS are visible only at 5-minute intervals, inflating mean-time-to-detect by up to 5×.

shortDescription.text: EC2 instance has detailed monitoring explicitly disabled

fullDescription.text: ECS task definition km_ecs_task: at least one container does not set readonlyRootFilesystem = true. Compromised containers can write payloads/persistence to the runtime image.

shortDescription.text: ECS task definition container does not enforce read-only root filesystem

fullDescription.text: ECS cluster does not enable Container Insights: cannot verify — required attribute(s) missing from source: setting

shortDescription.text: [Needs Review] ECS cluster does not enable Container Insights

fullDescription.text: ECS task definition km_ecs_task: task_role_arn is not set. The container falls back to the EC2 instance profile (or no role on Fargate) — both are wrong defaults.

shortDescription.text: ECS task definition is missing task_role_arn

fullDescription.text: ECS cluster km_ecs_cluster: execute-command configuration is missing — when operators run `aws ecs execute-command`, sessions are not logged. Investigations of insider misuse become unreliable.

shortDescription.text: ECS service does not enable execute-command logging

fullDescription.text: EKS cluster this: no encryption_config block. Kubernetes Secrets are stored unencrypted in etcd — anyone with etcd-level access (or backup access) reads them in plaintext.

shortDescription.text: EKS cluster does not enable Kubernetes secrets encryption

fullDescription.text: EKS cluster this: control-plane logging does not include `audit`. Forensic investigation of cluster-level access (kubectl, CRD operations, RBAC misuse) is impossible after the fact.

shortDescription.text: EKS cluster does not enable control-plane logging

fullDescription.text: Load balancer km_lb: access logging is not enabled. Request-level forensics (source IP, path, status, latency, backend) are unavailable after an incident — you cannot reconstruct who hit what.

shortDescription.text: Load balancer has no access logging

fullDescription.text: ALB km_lb: drop_invalid_header_fields is false. Malformed HTTP headers (e.g. header injection, request smuggling via ambiguous Content-Length) are forwarded to backend targets unchanged.

shortDescription.text: ALB does not drop invalid HTTP header fields

fullDescription.text: ALB km_lb: deletion protection is off. The load balancer can be deleted by any IAM principal with elasticloadbalancing:DeleteLoadBalancer — including a misconfigured CI pipeline or confused-deputy attack.

shortDescription.text: ALB has deletion protection disabled

fullDescription.text: ALB listener km_frontend_listener: protocol is HTTP and default action is not a redirect to HTTPS. Client connections are accepted over plaintext — session tokens, credentials, and API responses are transmitted unencrypted.

shortDescription.text: ALB listener uses HTTP without a redirect to HTTPS

fullDescription.text: EventBridge rule has Lambda target without aws_lambda_permission grant: cannot verify — required attribute(s) missing from source: name

shortDescription.text: [Needs Review] EventBridge rule has Lambda target without aws_lambda_permission grant

fullDescription.text: `aws_instance.web-app-instance` has a public IP and an IAM instance profile, the plan defines admin-equivalent IAM policy/policies (web-app-instance-policy, privileged-instance-policy), and there is NO WAF web ACL. An SSRF or RCE on this internet-reachable host reaches the instance metadata service, steals the admin-grade role credentials, and pivots to full account control — with no WAF in front to blunt the initial exploit. Each fact is individually common; together they form a complete breach chain.

shortDescription.text: Public-facing `aws_instance.web-app-instance` + admin IAM + no WAF — account-takeover path

fullDescription.text: IAM policy web-app-instance-policy: enables privilege escalation — set own IAM policy version; attach an administrator policy; add an inline admin policy; create access keys for another user; set a console login password; add a principal to a group; rewrite a role's trust policy; pass any role to a compute service; create a new role with admin policy; remove permissions boundary to escalate — granted on an unconstrained resource (Resource: "*") with no Condition. Any principal holding this policy can escalate to broader or administrator privileges.

shortDescription.text: IAM policy enables privilege escalation

fullDescription.text: Toxic combination on role web-app-instance-role: it is assumable by a compute service (EC2 / ECS / Lambda), AND its attached policies grant set own IAM policy version; attach an administrator policy; add an inline admin policy; create access keys for another user; set a console login password; add a principal to a group; rewrite a role's trust policy; pass any role to a compute service; create a new role with admin policy; remove permissions boundary to escalate. An attacker who compromises that workload (SSRF, RCE, dependency takeover) can steal the instance/task role credentials and obtain those permissions. Neither the trust policy nor the permission policy is flagged in isolation — the risk is the path between them.

shortDescription.text: IAM role reachable from a broad principal can escalate privileges

fullDescription.text: EC2 instance web-app-instance: IMDSv2 is not enforced (metadata_options.http_tokens ≠ "required") and the attached instance profile role `web-app-instance-role` grants set own IAM policy version; attach an administrator policy; add an inline admin policy; create access keys for another user; set a console login password; add a principal to a group; rewrite a role's trust policy; pass any role to a compute service; create a new role with admin policy; remove permissions boundary to escalate. Any server-side request forgery (SSRF) vulnerability in the running application can steal the role credentials in one unauthenticated HTTP request to 169.254.169.254, then immediately use them to set own IAM policy version; attach an administrator policy; add an inline admin policy; create access keys for another user; set a console login password; add a principal to a group; rewrite a role's trust policy; pass any role to a compute service; create a new role with admin policy; remove permissions boundary to escalate.

shortDescription.text: EC2 instance with IMDSv1 enabled and a privileged role enables SSRF → credential theft

fullDescription.text: IAM policy privileged-instance-policy: grants data exfiltration actions (s3:GetObject, dynamodb:Scan, dynamodb:GetItem, dynamodb:BatchGetItem, dynamodb:Query, kinesis:GetRecords, sqs:ReceiveMessage, rds:DownloadDBLogFilePortion, athena:GetQueryResults, glue:GetSecurityConfiguration) on Resource: "*" with no Condition. Any principal holding this policy can bulk-read or export data across all accessible resources of that service — without needing to escalate privileges first.

shortDescription.text: IAM policy grants bulk data exfiltration capability

fullDescription.text: IAM policy km_ssm_secrets_policy: grants credential extraction actions (secretsmanager:GetSecretValue, ssm:GetParameter, ssm:GetParameters, kms:Decrypt) on Resource: "*" with no Condition. Any principal holding this policy can read secrets, SSM parameters, or decrypt KMS-protected data across all resources in the account.

shortDescription.text: IAM policy grants credential/secret extraction capability

fullDescription.text: IAM policy privileged-instance-policy: grants resource-exposure actions (s3:PutBucketPolicy, s3:PutBucketAcl, s3:PutObjectAcl, lambda:AddPermission, lambda:AddLayerVersionPermission, sqs:AddPermission, sns:AddPermission, secretsmanager:PutResourcePolicy, kms:PutKeyPolicy, ecr:SetRepositoryPolicy) on Resource: "*" with no Condition. Any principal holding this policy can modify the access policy of S3 buckets, Lambda functions, SQS queues, KMS keys, or other resources — potentially making them publicly accessible.

shortDescription.text: IAM policy grants resource permission modification capability

fullDescription.text: Toxic combination on role km_ecs_task_execution_role: it is assumable by a compute service (EC2 / ECS / Lambda), AND its attached policies grant credential extraction actions (secretsmanager:GetSecretValue, ssm:GetParameter, ssm:GetParameters, kms:Decrypt) on Resource: "*" with no Condition. An attacker who compromises that workload (SSRF, RCE, dependency takeover) can steal the instance/task role credentials and obtain those permissions. An attacker who assumes this role can harvest every secret, SSM parameter, and KMS-encrypted value in the account in a single session.

shortDescription.text: Broadly-assumable IAM role with credential extraction capability

fullDescription.text: IAM policy web-app-instance-policy: grants infrastructure-modification actions (ec2:RunInstances, ec2:RequestSpotInstances, ec2:AuthorizeSecurityGroupIngress, ec2:CreateVpc, ec2:CreateKeyPair) on Resource: "*" with no Condition. Any principal holding this policy can launch EC2 instances, run ECS tasks, or modify network rules without restriction — a common cryptomining and lateral-movement vector.

shortDescription.text: IAM policy grants unscoped infrastructure launch/modification capability

fullDescription.text: KMS key km_db_kms_key: deletion_window_in_days is less than the maximum of 30. If scheduled for deletion, there is less time to cancel and recover data encrypted with this key.

shortDescription.text: KMS key deletion window should be the maximum (30 days)

fullDescription.text: ALB km_lb: no alarm references this load balancer. Target health is a load-balancer concern — without a paging hook, a target group running with 50% healthy hosts is silent until the last host fails.

shortDescription.text: ALB has no CloudWatch alarm on UnHealthyHostCount

fullDescription.text: RDS km_db: no alarms reference this instance. CPU saturation and connection-pool exhaustion are the two failure modes engineers don't catch until customers complain. Both have CloudWatch metrics; neither has a configured threshold here.

shortDescription.text: RDS instance has no CloudWatch alarm on CPU or DatabaseConnections

fullDescription.text: SQS queue this: no alarms reference this queue. Consumer death is invisible from the queue side until messages back up. ApproximateAgeOfOldestMessage exceeding a few minutes is unambiguous evidence the consumer stopped processing.

shortDescription.text: SQS queue has no alarm on ApproximateAgeOfOldestMessage

fullDescription.text: EKS cluster this: <= 1 aws_eks_node_group references this cluster. Single-pool clusters can't drain workloads across pools during a kernel upgrade or instance-family migration.

shortDescription.text: EKS cluster declares a single node group

fullDescription.text: ECS service km_ecs_service: desired_count = 1. No redundancy across tasks; rolling deploys are not actually rolling — they're sequential blackouts.

shortDescription.text: ECS service with desired_count = 1 (no redundancy)

fullDescription.text: RDS km_db: max_allocated_storage unset. The instance can't grow beyond its allocated_storage value. A surprise write spike → ENOSPC → forced downtime to bump storage manually.

shortDescription.text: RDS instance has no max_allocated_storage (no storage autoscaling)

fullDescription.text: IAM role km_ecs_task_execution_role: permissions_boundary unset. Future policy attachments can grant unlimited privilege — the role's effective permissions = union of all attached policies, no ceiling.

shortDescription.text: IAM role has no permissions_boundary

fullDescription.text: IAM policy km_ssm_secrets_policy: combines Resource: "*" with sensitive action prefixes. The blast radius of a token compromise is the entire account for those service classes.

shortDescription.text: IAM policy with privileged action + Resource: "*"

fullDescription.text: IAM policy km_ssm_secrets_policy: grants a high-privilege action with no Condition block. Compromise of any principal holding this policy gives unconstrained use of that action.

shortDescription.text: IAM policy grants privileged action with no Condition block

fullDescription.text: Elastic IP declared without instance / NAT / NLB association: cannot verify — required attribute(s) missing from source: domain

shortDescription.text: [Needs Review] Elastic IP declared without instance / NAT / NLB association

fullDescription.text: NAT Gateway km_nat_gateway: declared. If your plan has multiple of these in the same VPC (one per AZ), the redundancy costs $32/month per extra AZ — fine for prod with steady traffic, but commonly accidental in dev/staging.

shortDescription.text: Multiple NAT gateways in a small account (one shared NAT is enough)

fullDescription.text: EventBridge rule with rate(1 minute) schedule (high-cost cron): cannot verify — required attribute(s) missing from source: name

shortDescription.text: [Needs Review] EventBridge rule with rate(1 minute) schedule (high-cost cron)

fullDescription.text: mybucket: no lifecycle.prevent_destroy block. A terraform destroy or refactor that removes this resource address will delete it without a guardrail.

shortDescription.text: Stateful resource missing lifecycle { prevent_destroy = true }

fullDescription.text: Terraform required_providers: version = "~> 3.11" allows minor upgrades. Minor bumps in the AWS provider regularly introduce breaking changes.

shortDescription.text: Provider version uses too-loose constraint (~> N or pinned to major only)

fullDescription.text: this: uses `count = condition ? 1 : 0` pattern. Changes to the condition logic can renumber the resource's address, causing terraform to plan a delete+create instead of an update.

shortDescription.text: Resource uses `count = condition ? 1 : 0` (should be for_each)

fullDescription.text: AWS provider: no default_tags block. Every resource has to tag itself for Cost Explorer — teams forget on a third of them.

shortDescription.text: AWS provider block has no default_tags

fullDescription.text: Variable "default_tags": no `type` declaration and no validation block. Mistakes surface as cryptic provider errors instead of clear plan-time messages.

shortDescription.text: Variable declared without type or validation

fullDescription.text: VPC km_vpc: no aws_vpc_endpoint declared. Secrets Manager calls from in-VPC workloads traverse NAT. At ~$0.045/GB through NAT, even modest secret-fetching patterns pay back the endpoint cost in days.

shortDescription.text: VPC has no Secrets Manager VPC endpoint (Lambda → Secrets traffic via NAT)

fullDescription.text: VPC km_vpc: no aws_vpc_endpoint for KMS. Decrypt calls leave the VPC via NAT — for high-volume workloads this adds up faster than the endpoint cost.

shortDescription.text: VPC has no KMS VPC endpoint

fullDescription.text: Cluster km_ecs_cluster: no aws_vpc_endpoint declared for ECR or S3. Container image pulls + Docker layer downloads flow through NAT — common single-biggest contributor to NAT data-processing charges.

shortDescription.text: EKS / ECS cluster present but no ECR VPC endpoints

fullDescription.text: EKS cluster this: enabled_cluster_log_types missing `audit`. Cluster-level API access is invisible — no record of who called what during an incident or audit.

shortDescription.text: EKS cluster control plane logging types incomplete

fullDescription.text: km_ecs_cluster: no aws_inspector2_enabler in the plan. Container / runtime / EC2 CVEs aren't surfaced — the team is doing vulnerability management manually or not at all.

shortDescription.text: No aws_inspector2_enabler declared (no vulnerability scanning)

fullDescription.text: RDS instance should not be publicly accessible: cannot verify — required attribute(s) missing from source: publicly_accessible

shortDescription.text: [Needs Review] RDS instance should not be publicly accessible

fullDescription.text: RDS instance should have automated backups enabled (backup_retention_period > 0): cannot verify — required attribute(s) missing from source: backup_retention_period

shortDescription.text: [Needs Review] RDS instance should have automated backups enabled (backup_retention_period > 0)

fullDescription.text: RDS instance km_db has multi_az = false. A single-AZ failure will cause an outage for the duration of cross-AZ recovery (typically 30-90 minutes).

shortDescription.text: RDS production instance should have Multi-AZ enabled

fullDescription.text: RDS instance km_db: deletion_protection is disabled. An accidental terraform destroy will permanently delete the database.

shortDescription.text: RDS deletion protection should be enabled

fullDescription.text: S3 bucket example: at least one Block Public Access flag is disabled. Public-access risk is not fully mitigated.

shortDescription.text: S3 bucket should have Block Public Access enabled

fullDescription.text: S3 bucket policy on b does not reference aws:SecureTransport — traffic can potentially be accepted over plain HTTP.

shortDescription.text: S3 bucket policy should deny non-TLS (HTTP) access

fullDescription.text: S3 bucket mybucket: no aws_s3_bucket_public_access_block references this bucket. Public access posture is one mis-applied policy or ACL away.

shortDescription.text: S3 bucket has no Block Public Access companion resource

fullDescription.text: SQS queue this: no redrive_policy declared. Messages that fail to process retry until message_retention_seconds expires, then drop silently.

shortDescription.text: SQS queue has no DLQ — failed messages drop silently

fullDescription.text: VPC km_vpc: no aws_flow_log resource references this VPC. Network-level forensics (lateral movement, exfiltration) are unreliable post-incident.

shortDescription.text: VPC has no associated flow logs

fullDescription.text: Security group km_rds_sg: ingress allows 0.0.0.0/0 on a known debug/management port (e.g., 8080 / 5432 / 9200 / 6379). These are admin-tool defaults — exposing them to the internet is almost always unintended.

shortDescription.text: Security group permits 0.0.0.0/0 on a known debug or management port

fullDescription.text: Subnet km_public_subnet: map_public_ip_on_launch = true. Every instance launched into this subnet receives a public IP automatically — including future instances created by ASGs, ECS, or EKS without an explicit private-only override.

shortDescription.text: VPC subnet automatically assigns public IPs on launch

fullDescription.text: Role is declared but no other resource references it. Either delete it or document why it's kept (e.g., AssumeRole from another account).

shortDescription.text: IAM role web-app-instance-role has no consumers

fullDescription.text: Security group is declared but no resource attaches it. Likely a leftover from a deleted service.

shortDescription.text: Security group km_ecs_sg has no consumers
